---
title: "Causal Inference for Policy Analysis"
author: "Will Horne"
format: revealjs
editor: visual
code-fold: true
execute:
  fig-align: center
---



## Causation

::: incremental
-   You have surely heard "Correlation is not causation" before

    -   True enough, but also a sort of lazy criticism of quantitative research

-   Last \~20-30 years, incredible effort to isolate and measure credible causal effects

-   That will be the focus of the second half of the class

    -   But first let's look at some funny comics
:::

## Comic

![](images/Causal.png){fig-align="center" width="100%"}

## Comic

![](images/Cellphones.png){fig-align="center"}

## Comic

![](images/Controls.png){fig-align="center"}

## What the rest of the semester will be

![](images/Charlie.png){fig-align="center" width="100%"}

## Why Causal Inference?

::: incremental
-   Pre 'Credibility Revolution' social science

    -   Garbage can regression: throw everything in there and hope for the best

        -   A good chance that, if you were an undergrad social science major, this is what you learned!

        -   But...not even clear what we are estimating?

-   Let's look at an example
:::

## The Kitchen Sink: Head Start {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   You want to know: does Head Start improve kids' test scores?

-   Start simple: regress test scores on Head Start enrollment

-   But Head Start kids come from lower-income families. Better add income as a control. Coefficient changes.

-   Maybe parent education matters too. Add it. Coefficient changes again.

-   Neighborhood quality? Race? Family structure? Number of siblings?

-   Each time you add a variable, the coefficient moves. Which number is the "right" one?
:::

## Why Is This a Problem? {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   **Problem 1:** You have no principled way to decide what belongs in the model. You are just adding variables until the result looks reasonable (or publishable).

-   **Problem 2:** Each control changes what your coefficient *means*

    -   No controls: comparing Head Start kids to everyone else

    -   Control for income: comparing Head Start kids to non-Head Start kids *at the same income level*

    -   Control for income and parent education: now you're comparing kids whose parents have the same income *and* education

    -   These are all different questions! The more controls you add, the harder it is to say what you're actually estimating.
:::

## We Need a Framework {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   Causal inference gives you a way to decide *which* variables belong in your model and *why*

-   Instead of guessing, you start from a model of how the world works and let that guide your analysis

-   Data + Assumptions = Conclusions

    -   If you assume families enroll in Head Start randomly, a simple comparison of means is fine

    -   If you think lower-income families are more likely to enroll, you need a different strategy entirely

    -   Same data, totally different conclusions depending on what you assume about how people end up in the program
:::

## Why Should MPAs Care?

::: incremental
-   Policy reports you read will use this language: ATE, counterfactual, selection bias

-   Grant proposals and program evaluations require causal reasoning

-   Understanding these ideas helps you be a **better consumer** of evidence, even if you never run a regression again

-   And if you do run regressions, this framework tells you *when to trust them*
:::

## Causal Inference Readings

-   Different than first part of the semester on specific statistical methods

-   More applied readings, posted to Canvas

-   Two very user friendly books

    -   Honestly fun reads, almost breezy for academic books

    -   Cunningham: Grad(+) level text, Potential Outcomes

    -   Huntington-Klein: Upper undergrad/Intro grad level, DAGs

## Today's Roadmap

::: incremental
1.  Counterfactuals and Potential Outcomes
2.  Average Treatment Effects
3.  Estimands
4.  DAGs
5.  Identification
:::

# Part 1: Counterfactuals

## What is causation?

::: incremental
-   How would you define causation? How do you know that **A *causes* B?**

-   Mill (1843): "If a person eats a particular dish, and dies...and would not have died had he not eaten it...the dish was the source of his death"

-   Today we formalize this intuition using counterfactuals and DAGs

    -   Causation can be probabilistic, not deterministic

    -   Treatments can have heterogeneous effects
:::

## Counterfactual Thinking {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   Does increasing the minimum wage increase unemployment?

    -   We observe that after the minimum wage is raised, unemployment goes up

    -   But unemployment was already trending up. And the economy was slowing down.

    -   So was it the minimum wage, or would unemployment have risen anyway?

    -   **Counterfactual**: What *would have happened* to unemployment without the minimum wage increase?

-   Does having a daughter make judges more likely to give pro-choice rulings?

    -   A judge with a daughter gives a pro-choice ruling

    -   **Counterfactual:** What *would that same judge have ruled* with no daughter?
:::

## The Key Idea

A **counterfactual** is the answer to: "What would have happened if things had been different?"

Every causal claim is really a comparison between what *did* happen and what *would have* happened under a different scenario.

. . .

We can never directly observe the counterfactual. That's the central challenge of all causal inference.

## The Fundamental Problem of Causal Inference

::: incremental
-   Holland (1986) identifies the fundamental problem of causal inference:

    -   **For each unit, we only observe one potential outcome**

    -   We either see what happened with the treatment, or without it. Never both.

-   So how can we make inferences about the outcome we *didn't* see?

-   The rest of this course is about strategies for solving this problem
:::

# Part 2: The Potential Outcomes Framework

## Example: Political Canvassing {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   Study *n* voters

    -   $n_{1}$ are canvassed (visited by door knockers)

    -   $n_{0}$ = $n - n_{1}$ are not canvassed

-   For each voter $i$, we observe an outcome

    -   $Y_{i}$ = Turnout (did they vote?)

    -   Treatment Variable: $$D_i =
        \begin{cases}
        1 & \text{if treated (canvassed)} \\
        0 & \text{if control (not canvassed)}
        \end{cases} $$
:::

## Potential Outcomes Notation

Potential outcomes give structure to our counterfactuals (Neyman 1923, Rubin \~ 1975)

::: incremental
-   $Y_{i}(1)$: outcome that unit *i* **would have** if treated

-   $Y_{i}(0)$: outcome that unit *i* **would have** if not treated
:::

## The Causal Effect

The **causal effect** for unit *i* is simply the difference between the two potential outcomes:

$$\tau_i = Y_i(1) - Y_i(0)$$

. . .

How much better (or worse) off is this person because they received the treatment? We can **never observe this** for any individual, because we only see one of the two potential outcomes.

## What We Get to See

| Voter | Age $X_{i}$ | Contact $D_i$ | Turnout $Y_i(1)$ | Turnout $Y_i(0)$ | Causal effect $Y_i(1) - Y_i(0)$ |
|------------|------------|------------|------------|------------|------------|
| 1 | 25 | 1 | 0 | ??? | ??? |
| 2 | 38 | 0 | ??? | 1 | ??? |
| 3 | 67 | 0 | ??? | 0 | ??? |
| ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ | ⋮ |
| n | 43 | 1 | 1 | ??? | ??? |

The "???" cells are the fundamental problem. We never get to fill them in.

## A Worked Example: Surgery vs Chemo {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   Imagine a certain type of cancer has two treatments: surgery or chemotherapy

    -   Surgery: $D_{i} = 1$ ; Chemo: $D_{i} = 0$

    -   Outcome $Y_{i}$: years lived post treatment

-   Imagine that (by some miracle) we had potential outcome data for ten patients

    -   $E[Y^{1}] = 5.6$, $E[Y^{0}] = 5$

    -   So the **true ATE** $= 0.6$ years, or about 7 months on average
:::

## The Full Potential Outcomes Table

::: {style="font-size: 70%;"}
| Patient | $Y^S$ (Surgery) | $Y^C$ (Chemo) | $\delta = Y^S - Y^C$ |
|---------|-----------------|---------------|----------------------|
| 1       | 7               | 1             | 6                    |
| 2       | 5               | 6             | -1                   |
| 3       | 5               | 1             | 4                    |
| 4       | 7               | 8             | -1                   |
| 5       | 4               | 2             | 2                    |
| 6       | 10              | 1             | 9                    |
| 7       | 1               | 10            | -9                   |
| 8       | 5               | 6             | -1                   |
| 9       | 3               | 7             | -4                   |
| 10      | 9               | 8             | 1                    |
:::

Notice that the treatment effect is **different for every patient.** Some benefit a lot from surgery; others are harmed by it.

## But Here's What We Actually See

::: {style="font-size: 60%;"}
| Patient | $Y$ | $D$ |
|---------|-----|-----|
| 1       | 7   | 1   |
| 2       | 6   | 0   |
| 3       | 5   | 1   |
| 4       | 8   | 0   |
| 5       | 4   | 1   |
| 6       | 10  | 1   |
| 7       | 10  | 0   |
| 8       | 6   | 0   |
| 9       | 7   | 0   |
| 10      | 9   | 1   |
:::

A doctor assigns each patient to whatever treatment seems "best" for them.

## Try It Yourself

::: incremental
-   Surgery patients (D=1): 7, 5, 4, 10, 9. Average = **7.0**

-   Chemo patients (D=0): 6, 8, 10, 6, 7. Average = **7.4**

-   Naive estimate: 7.0 - 7.4 = **-0.4**

-   But the true ATE was **+0.6**. We're not just wrong, we got the *sign* wrong.

-   The naive estimate says surgery *hurts*. The truth is surgery *helps*.

-   What went wrong?
:::

## The Switching Equation: Intuition First {.smaller}

Now that we've seen the surgery data, let's formalize what's going on. Go back to the full table and look at Patient 3. She got surgery, so $D_i = 1$.

::: incremental
-   We see her surgery outcome: $Y_i(1) = 5$ years

-   We do *not* see what would have happened with chemo: $Y_i(0) = ???$

-   Now look at Patient 4. He got chemo, so $D_i = 0$.

-   We see his chemo outcome: $Y_i(0) = 8$ years

-   We do *not* see his surgery outcome: $Y_i(1) = ???$
:::

## The Switching Equation: The Formula

$$Y_{i} = D_{i} \cdot Y_{i}(1) + (1 - D_{i}) \cdot Y_{i}(0)$$

. . .

Plug in $D_i = 1$: $Y_i = 1 \cdot Y_i(1) + 0 \cdot Y_i(0) = Y_i(1)$. We see the surgery outcome.

. . .

Plug in $D_i = 0$: $Y_i = 0 \cdot Y_i(1) + 1 \cdot Y_i(0) = Y_i(0)$. We see the chemo outcome.

. . .

The other potential outcome is always hidden from us.

## Average Treatment Effects

The **average treatment effect (ATE)** is the average causal effect across all units:

$$ATE = E[Y_{i}(1)] - E[Y_{i}(0)]$$

. . .

If we could give **everyone** the treatment and also give **everyone** the control, and compare, what would the average difference be? We can never calculate this directly, but we can try to estimate it.

## Average Treatment Effect on the Treated (ATT) {.smaller}

Sometimes we focus only on those who actually received the treatment:

$$ATT = E[Y_{i}(1) - Y_{i}(0) \mid D_{i} = 1]$$

. . .

Among the people who actually got treated, how much did the treatment help them on average?

. . .

There's also an **ATU** (Average Treatment Effect on the Untreated). Same logic, but for those who *didn't* receive treatment.

## Why ATE and ATT Can Be Very Different {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   A job training program has an ATE of \$2,000 in extra annual earnings

-   But the people who actually signed up were the most motivated job seekers. For them, the effect was \$4,000. That's the ATT.

-   If you expand the program to everyone, don't expect \$4,000. The less motivated participants will probably benefit less.

-   This matters for policy: the effect you measure on *volunteers* may not generalize to the full population you want to serve
:::

## Why Can't We Just Compare Averages? {.smaller}

What we *want* is the ATE. What we can *calculate* from data is the difference in observed outcomes between the treated and untreated groups.

. . .

$$\underbrace{E[Y|D = 1] - E[Y|D = 0]}_{\text{What we observe}} = \underbrace{ATE}_{\text{What we want}} + \underbrace{E[Y^{0}|D = 1] - E[Y^{0}|D = 0]}_{\text{Selection Bias}} + \underbrace{(1 - \pi)(ATT - ATU)}_{\text{Heterogeneous Effect Bias}}$$

. . .

Three pieces: the thing we want, and two sources of bias that contaminate it.

## Bias in the Surgery Example {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   **Selection Bias**: $E[Y^{0}|D = 1] - E[Y^{0}|D = 0]$

    -   The surgery patients were sicker to begin with. Even *without* surgery, they would have lived fewer years than the chemo patients.

    -   $E[Y^0 | D=1] < E[Y^0 | D=0]$, so this term is negative. It drags our naive estimate down.

-   **Heterogeneous Effect Bias**: $(1-\pi)(ATT - ATU)$

    -   Surgery helps sick patients more than it would help healthy ones. The people who got surgery (ATT) benefit more than the people who didn't (ATU).

    -   So $ATT \neq ATU$, and because treatment was assigned to a non-random group, this creates additional bias.

-   Together, these two biases flipped our estimate from +0.6 to -0.4.
:::

## So How Do We Fix This?

::: incremental
-   The problem is that who gets treated is tangled up with the potential outcomes

-   The doctor assigned sicker patients to surgery. So the treated group and the control group were different *before* treatment even started.

-   If we could somehow make treatment assignment have nothing to do with the patients' potential outcomes, both bias terms would disappear

-   When is that possible?
:::

## The Independence Assumption

If the treatment is *independent of the potential outcomes*:

$$(Y^{1}, Y^{0}) \perp D_{i}$$

. . .

This means that who gets treated has nothing to do with how they would respond to treatment. If this holds, both bias terms vanish and the simple comparison of averages **is** the ATE.

. . .

When is this realistic? **Randomized experiments or Exogenous Variation.** That's basically it. Everything else requires assumptions.

## SUTVA {.smaller}

The other key assumption: **Stable Unit Treatment Value Assumption**

::: incremental
-   Each unit receives the same "dose" of treatment

-   No **spillover** to other units' potential outcomes

-   No **general equilibrium** effects

-   **Policy examples of SUTVA violations:**

    -   Vaccinating one person protects their neighbors (spillover)
    -   A job training program helps participants but crowds out non-participants (general equilibrium)
    -   Minimum wage increases affect untreated workers through price changes

-   If violated, we're not hopeless. There are strategies we'll cover later
:::

# Part 3: Setting the Target

## What Are You Actually Estimating?

::: incremental
-   We said Head Start "works." But works for *whom*?

    -   All kids? Just low-income kids? Kids in rural areas? Kids whose parents would not have sent them to any preschool otherwise?

-   The answer changes depending on who you're studying and what comparison you're making

-   A lot of applied research skips this step. People run a regression and interpret the coefficient without being clear about what real-world quantity it's supposed to represent.
:::

## The Estimand {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   An **estimand** is your target quantity. It is the thing your study is trying to learn about the world.

-   It has two parts:

    1.  A **unit-specific quantity**: what is the causal effect for a single person or case? For example: "If this child attended Head Start, how much higher would their test score be compared to not attending?"
    2.  A **target population**: who are you averaging over? All children in the US? Only income-eligible children? Only children in the states you have data for?

-   You need to define both of these *before* you pick a model. Your research design, your controls, and your data all need to line up with your estimand. If they don't, you might get a precise answer to the wrong question.
:::

## Why This Matters: Head Start Again {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   Suppose you want the ATE of Head Start on test scores for all income-eligible children

-   But your data only covers children in three states, and you use a regression that controls for parent education

-   Now think about what you're actually estimating:

    -   Your target population is not "all income-eligible children." It's "income-eligible children in these three states."

    -   By controlling for parent education, you're comparing Head Start kids to non-Head Start kids *whose parents have the same education*. That is a narrower comparison than what you started with.

-   None of this is necessarily wrong, but you need to be honest about it. The estimand you *wanted* and the estimand you *got* may not be the same thing.
:::

## Example: Pager (2003) {.smaller}

Pager (2003) studied how race and criminal record affect employment outcomes.

::: incremental
-   She sent pairs of fake job applications to real entry-level job postings in Milwaukee

-   The applicants were either Black or White, and either had a felony conviction listed or not

-   Everything else about the applications was kept the same: qualifications, work history, cover letter

-   This is called an **audit study**. Because the researcher controls what the employer sees, any difference in callbacks must be caused by what was varied on the application.
:::

## Why Pager Is a Model Estimand {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   **Unit-specific quantity:** For a given job posting, does listing a felony conviction reduce the chance of getting a callback? And does the size of that penalty differ by race?

-   **Target population:** Entry-level job postings in Milwaukee

-   **Comparison:** Black applicant with a record vs. Black applicant without one. White applicant with a record vs. White applicant without one. And then: how do those two penalties compare?

-   Notice how specific this is. There is no ambiguity about what Pager is estimating, who she is estimating it for, or what comparison she is making. That is what a good estimand looks like.

-   Also notice what she is *not* claiming. She is not saying this is the effect for all jobs in the US, or for high-skill positions, or in cities with different labor markets. The estimand matches the data.
:::

## Pager's Results {.smaller}

![](images/Race%20and%20Felonies.png){fig-align="center" width="100%"}

Because Pager was clear about her estimand, we know exactly what these bars mean.

## What Did Pager Find? {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   A criminal record reduced callbacks for both races, but the penalty was much larger for Black applicants

-   White applicants with a felony record received callbacks about 17% of the time. White applicants without a record received callbacks about 34% of the time.

-   Black applicants with a felony record received callbacks about 5% of the time. Black applicants without a record received callbacks about 14% of the time.

-   Put differently: White applicants *with* a criminal record were called back at roughly the same rate as Black applicants *without* one

-   This is a striking finding, and it is only possible to interpret clearly because the estimand was so well defined. We know exactly who was studied, what was compared, and what was held constant.

-   We don't overclaim. These results may not generalize to labor markets with different dynamics.
:::

## Example: Angrist and Evans (1998) {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   Question: does having a third child reduce women's labor supply?

-   You might try comparing women with two kids to women with three. But the decision to have a third child is not random. Women who choose to have three kids are probably different in ways that also affect whether they work.

-   So a simple comparison would mix up the effect of the third child with all the reasons some women have more children in the first place. Selection bias, just like the surgery example.

-   Angrist and Evans needed a way around this. Their solution used what's called an **instrumental variable**.
:::

## What Is an Instrument? (30,000 ft View) {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   An **instrument** is something that pushes people toward treatment but has no direct effect on the outcome

-   Think of it as a natural nudge. It changes whether someone gets treated, but it doesn't affect the outcome through any other channel.

-   The idea is: find something that creates random-ish variation in who gets treated, and use that variation to estimate the causal effect.

-   Angrist and Evans noticed that parents whose first two children are the same sex (two boys or two girls) are more likely to have a third child. So "same sex among first two children" is the instrument.

-   It nudges families toward having a third child, but it has no direct reason to affect whether the mother works.
:::

## How the Instrument Narrows the Estimand {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   Here is the catch. The instrument only affects a specific group of families: those who would have a third child *because* their first two were the same sex, but would not have had a third child otherwise.

-   Families who were always going to have three kids regardless? The instrument didn't change their behavior. Families who were never going to have a third child no matter what? Same thing.

-   So the causal effect Angrist and Evans estimated only applies to that middle group, the families on the margin. In the causal inference literature, these are called **compliers**.

-   That is a very specific slice of the population. If you want to use this result to design family policy, you need to ask: does the effect for these marginal families generalize to everyone else? It might not.

-   The method they chose determined who they could learn about. The estimand followed from the research design, not the other way around.
:::

## The Takeaway on Estimands

::: incremental
-   Define your question as a real-world quantity first, then pick a method that can credibly answer it. Not the other way around.

-   Be specific about *who* your estimate applies to. "The effect of X on Y" is not enough. The effect for whom? Compared to what?

-   Your research design, your data, and your controls all shape what you end up estimating. If you're not deliberate about this, you can end up with a precise answer to a question nobody asked.
:::

# Part 4: DAGs

## What Are DAGs? {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   **Directed Acyclic Graphs**: a visual tool for laying out your causal model

    -   **Directed**: arrows show the direction of causation

    -   **Acyclic**: no feedback loops (A can't cause B which causes A)

-   Useful for:

    -   Making your assumptions explicit
    -   Figuring out what to control for
    -   Justifying your research design to readers and reviewers
:::

## DAGs in R {.smaller}

We'll use the `ggdag` package, which builds on ggplot syntax you already know.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(ggdag)

# dagify() takes formulas: outcome ~ cause1 + cause2
# coords set the layout: x goes left to right, y goes bottom to top
dag <- dagify(
  Scores ~ Size + Funding,
  Size ~ Funding,
  exposure = "Funding",
  outcome = "Scores",
  coords = list(
    x = c(Funding = 1, Size = 2, Scores = 3),
    y = c(Funding = 0, Size = 1, Scores = 0)
  )
)

ggdag(dag, text = FALSE, use_labels = "name") +
  theme_dag()
```

::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture10_CausalInference_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-2-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


## What Goes in a DAG?

::: incremental
-   Focus on the **important stuff**. Most beginners put too much in their DAGs!

-   Start with **all causes of your outcome**

-   Anything your outcome causes can usually be ignored

-   You are making *assumptions* about relationships when you draw (or don't draw) an arrow

    -   This should be based on careful thought, theory, and prior research

-   In a few slides we'll build a DAG together that has way too much in it, and then we'll practice simplifying
:::

## The Three Building Blocks {.smaller}

Every DAG is built from three simple structures. Understanding these is the key to knowing what to control for.

. . .

**1. The Chain (Mediation):** A → B → C

If you want the *total* effect of A on C, **don't** control for B.

. . .

**2. The Fork (Confounding):** A ← C → B

C creates a spurious association between A and B. **Control for C** to remove the bias.

. . .

**3. The Collider:** A → C ← B

A and B are independent. But if you **control for C**, you create a false association between A and B. *Don't* control for colliders!

## Chain Example: Slippery Sidewalks


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture10_CausalInference_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


## Fork Example: Poverty as a Confounder {.smaller}


::: {.cell layout-align="center"}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture10_CausalInference_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-4-1.png){fig-align='center' width=768}
:::
:::


Poverty causes both poor health and low educational attainment. If you don't account for poverty, it looks like education causes health. Control for poverty to break the spurious link.

## Collider Example: PhD Admissions {.smaller}

:::{style="text-align: center;"}

::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture10_CausalInference_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-5-1.png){width=70%}
:::
:::

:::

. . .

::: callout-warning
## The Collider Trap

Research Experience → Admission ← GRE Score. Among all applicants, GRE and research experience are unrelated. But if you only look at *admitted students* (conditioning on the collider), you'll find a negative correlation, because admitted students with low GRE scores must have had great research experience, and vice versa.
:::

## Unobserved Variables {.smaller}

We use $U$ for variables we can't measure but believe exist. These are often the biggest headache for identification. Remember our estimands discussion: if U is unobserved, you can't control for it, and what you end up estimating may not be what you intended.


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture10_CausalInference_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-6-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


## Building a DAG: Online Classes and Dropout {.smaller}

Imagine we want to know: **Does taking online classes make students more likely to drop out?**

What variables matter? Let's think through it together...

## Building a DAG: The Result


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture10_CausalInference_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-7-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


## Simplifying DAGs

::: incremental
-   If effect sizes are likely very small, probably safe to remove

-   If variables have the same arrows in and out, group them together

-   If B only connects to C through A → B → C, we can simplify to A → C

-   If a variable isn't on any path between treatment and outcome, remove it
:::

## Simplified Version


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture10_CausalInference_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-8-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


## What About Feedback Loops?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture10_CausalInference_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-9-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


This is **not** a DAG. Everything causes everything else. Solution: use **time** to break the cycle.

## Using Time to Break Cycles


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture10_CausalInference_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-10-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


Example from Huntington-Klein. Once we add time subscripts, the cycle becomes a chain!

# Part 5: Using DAGs for Identification

## Paths

::: incremental
-   A **path** is any route between two variables, following arrows in *either* direction

-   You don't have to follow the arrow direction to trace a path

-   Variables may be connected by **multiple paths**

-   To figure out whether your estimate is biased, you need to identify *all* paths between treatment and outcome
:::

## How Many Paths? {.smaller}

Let's practice on a simple DAG before we apply this to real examples.


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture10_CausalInference_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-11-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


How many paths are there from A to C?

## Front-Door and Back-Door Paths {.smaller}

::: incremental
-   **Front-door (good) paths**: arrows flow from treatment → outcome. These represent the causal effect you want to measure.

    -   Direct paths and mediated paths count as front-door

-   **Back-door (bad) paths**: at least one arrow points *toward* the treatment. These represent confounding, i.e., alternative explanations for why treatment and outcome are correlated.

-   **Your goal**: close all back-door paths (by controlling for the right variables) while leaving front-door paths open
:::

## Confounding


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture10_CausalInference_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-12-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


C is a **confounder**: it causes both D and Y. If we don't control for C, the D→Y path is contaminated by the back-door path through C.

## Unobserved Confounding (The Bad Place) {.smaller}


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture10_CausalInference_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-13-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


If we **can't** control for U (because we can't observe it), our estimates of D→Y will be biased. This is when we need the design-based approaches we'll learn starting next week: experiments, DiD, RDD, IV.

## Confounding Example: Education and Earnings


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture10_CausalInference_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-14-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


How many paths from Education to Earnings? What should we control for to get the causal effect?

## Two Strategies for Identification {.smaller}


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture10_CausalInference_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-15-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


. . .

**Strategy 1:** Control for **O** (adjust for all causes of the outcome)

. . .

**Strategy 2:** Control for **C** (balance the determinants of the treatment)

. . .

Both close the back-door path. Different strategies, same goal.

## Watch Out for Colliders {.smaller}

What do we need to condition on to get the effect of D on Y?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture10_CausalInference_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-16-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


. . .

C is a **collider**. Controlling for it would *open* a back-door path rather than close one!

## A Harder Example {.smaller}


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output-display}
![](PADM8410Lecture10_CausalInference_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-17-1.png){width=960}
:::
:::


Assume **U** and **V** are unobservable. Can we identify the effect of D on Y? What should we control for?

## Why Draw DAGs? {.smaller}

Drawing a DAG forces you to think about identification *before* you run a regression. It might save you from a serious mistake, or convince you that your research design is sound.

. . .

::: callout-important
## Charles Manski (1994)

**Negative identification** findings imply that statistical inference is fruitless: it makes no sense to try to use a sample of finite size to infer something that could not be learned even if the sample were of infinite size. **Positive identification** findings imply that one should go on...(ie, do research, collect data, pick appropriate models)
:::

# Wrapping Up

## Key Takeaways {.smaller}

This was a dense lecture. Here are the ideas to hold onto:

::: incremental
1.  Causal claims are really about **counterfactuals**: what would have happened under a different scenario. We never observe the counterfactual directly.

2.  Naive comparisons between treated and untreated groups can be badly misleading because of **selection bias**. The surgery example showed us getting the sign wrong.

3.  Before you run anything, be clear about your **estimand**: the effect of what, for whom, compared to what alternative?

4.  **DAGs** are a tool for making your assumptions visible and figuring out what you need to control for. Chains, forks, and colliders each have different rules.
:::

## What's Next

::: incremental
-   Next week: **Fixed effects**, using panel data to control for unobserved confounders

-   Then: **Design-based causal inference**

    -   Experiments (Natural, Survey, Lab)
    -   Difference-in-Differences
    -   Regression Discontinuity
:::

## For Your Own Work

::: callout-tip
## Think About This Now

What is your **estimand**? Can you draw a **DAG** for your research question? What are the biggest threats to identification? Even if you're doing descriptive work, these tools help you think clearly about what your analysis can and can't tell us.
:::

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
